metadata.description: ETL DataFrames Knowledge Base for Agent Guidance

etl_dataframes.name: df_combined_dataframe
etl_dataframes.description: TO BE FILLED

etl_dataframes.name: df_preprocessed
etl_dataframes.description: Statistical summary of all numeric metrics in the trace (mean, std, min, max, percentiles)

columns.metric: Name of measurement (CPU numbers, frequencies, WLC, etc.)

retrieval_code: # Check for anomalies - large std deviation
use_cases: Quick statistical overview of entire trace
Check min/max/mean values for any metric
Identify outliers or anomalies in data ranges
Baseline comparison between traces

etl_dataframes.name: df_PPM_behaviour
etl_dataframes.description: Dump of PPM (Power Performance Manager) settings captured from ETL trace

columns.actual_value: The actual value found in the trace
columns.expected_value: Expected value from constraints file (usually empty)
columns.match: Boolean indicating if actual matches expected (usually null)

retrieval_code: # Compare DC vs AC for same parameter
use_cases: Check what PPM settings are configured on the system
Verify performance increase/decrease thresholds
Compare DC (battery) vs AC (plugged in) power mode settings
Check P-core (_0) vs E-core (_1) policy differences
Debug CPU performance scaling behavior
etl_dataframes.notes:
PPM setting name format: Default_{Parameter}_{PowerMode}_{CoreType}
- Parameter: PerfDecreaseTime, PerfIncreaseThreshold, PerfMinPolicy, etc.
- PowerMode: DC (battery) or AC (plugged in)
- CoreType: 0 (P-cores/Performance), 1 (E-cores/Efficient)

etl_dataframes.name: df_PPM_Validation
etl_dataframes.description: Same as df_PPM_behaviour but validated against different constraints file

columns.actual_value: The actual value found in the trace
columns.expected_value: Expected value from validation constraints file
columns.match: Boolean indicating if actual matches expected

retrieval_code: # Check for mismatches if validation constraints were loaded
use_cases: Validate PPM settings against known-good baseline
Same as df_PPM_behaviour but with validation-specific constraints
Check for configuration mismatches

etl_dataframes.name: df_heteroresponse
etl_dataframes.description: Metrics for debugging heterogeneous CPU systems (P-core vs E-core scheduling decisions)

columns.timestamp: Time in seconds when hetero scheduling decision was made
columns.EstimatedUtility: Predicted utilization number by the system
columns.decision: Binary indicator (1 = threshold crossed for zone transition - hybrid to crossover to perf zone)

retrieval_code: # Histogram or value counts for decision bit
# Get time intervals where decision = 1 (threshold crossed)
use_cases: Debug heterogeneous CPU scheduling behavior (P-core vs E-core)
Analyze system's ability to predict workload utility
Check zone transitions (hybrid -> crossover -> performance zones)
Identify when workloads cross thresholds requiring core migration
Compare estimated vs actual utilization for prediction accuracy
Find time periods with scheduling decisions for follow-up analysis
etl_dataframes.notes: decision bit = 1 indicates threshold has been crossed for moving to next performance zone\nZones: Hybrid zone -> Crossover zone -> Performance zone

etl_dataframes.name: df_cpu_util
etl_dataframes.description: CPU utilization percentage per logical processor over time
automatic_retrieval: # Collect basic statistics across all CPUs on ETL load
cpu_cols = [col for col in df_cpu_util.columns if col != 'timestamp']
# Return summary statistics for all CPUs

columns.0_to_15: CPU utilization percentage for each logical processor (column name is CPU number)

retrieval_code: # Get average utilization across all CPUs
cpu_cols = [str(i) for i in range(16)]  # Adjust based on system
# Find time periods with high CPU usage (>80%)
# P-core vs E-core analysis (example: CPUs 0-3 are P-cores, 4-15 are E-cores)
# Get utilization in specific time window
# Find which CPU had highest utilization
use_cases: Analyze CPU load patterns over time
Identify which cores are being utilized
Compare P-core vs E-core workload distribution
Find time periods with high/low CPU activity
Debug CPU bottlenecks or imbalances
Check if workloads are properly distributed across cores
etl_dataframes.notes: CPU numbering depends on system configuration\nTypical hybrid system: Lower numbered CPUs are often P-cores, higher are E-cores\nAutomatic stats collected on load for quick overview without full dataframe

etl_dataframes.name: df_softparkselection
etl_dataframes.description: Soft park core selection decisions - which CPUs are being parked or unparked over time
automatic_retrieval: # Collect histogram of NewPark values (categorical) as percentage
# Return park state distribution in percentage

columns.OldPark: Binary bitmask of CPUs parked before (not commonly used)
columns.NewPark: Binary bitmask of CPUs currently parked (main analysis column)
columns.NewSoftPark: Binary bitmask of soft-parked CPUs (not commonly used)

retrieval_code: # Count how many CPUs are parked at each timestamp
lambda x: bin(int(x, 2)).count('1') if isinstance(x, str) else 0
# Find parking transitions (when park state changed)
# Check specific parking pattern (e.g., if E-cores are parked)
# Example: 0b1111111111110000 means CPUs 4-15 parked, CPUs 0-3 active
use_cases: Analyze CPU parking behavior and patterns
Count how many CPUs are parked over time
Find parking state transitions
Check if E-cores are being parked appropriately
Verify parking policy is working correctly
Debug issues with core parking decisions
etl_dataframes.notes:
NewPark is the primary column for analysis
Binary bitmask format: each bit represents a CPU (1=parked, 0=active)
Example: 0b1111111111110000 = CPUs 0-3 active, CPUs 4-15 parked
Automatic histogram collected on load shows parking state distribution

etl_dataframes.name: df_ppm_settings
etl_dataframes.description: Raw baseline PPM (Power Performance Manager) settings extracted from ETL trace - PRIMARY dataframe for PPM analysis

columns.value_decimal: The decimal value of the setting

retrieval_code: # Compare DC vs AC for same parameter
# Get all settings for specific parameter
use_cases: Primary dataframe for checking PPM settings on the system
Verify performance increase/decrease thresholds
Compare DC (battery) vs AC (plugged in) power mode settings
Check P-core (_0) vs E-core (_1) policy differences
Debug CPU performance scaling behavior
Validate system configuration
etl_dataframes.notes:
This is the PRIMARY dataframe for PPM analysis - agents should use this instead of df_PPM_behaviour
PPM setting name format: Default_{Parameter}_{PowerMode}_{CoreType}
- Parameter: PerfDecreaseTime, PerfIncreaseThreshold, PerfMinPolicy, etc.
- PowerMode: DC (battery) or AC (plugged in)
- CoreType: 0 (P-cores/Performance), 1 (E-cores/Efficient)

etl_dataframes.name: df_c0_intervals
etl_dataframes.description: ACPI C-state intervals for each CPU - tracks when CPUs are in different power states (C0=active, C1/C2/C3=sleep states). LARGE dataset (700K+ rows)

columns.Start(s): Start time of the C-state interval in seconds
columns.End(s): End time of the C-state interval in seconds
columns.Duration(s): Duration of the interval in seconds

retrieval_code: # Calculate C0 residency percentage for specific CPU between time intervals
# Filter for specific CPU and time range
# Sum up all durations for this state
# Calculate all C-states for a CPU in time window
# Get C0 residency for all CPUs in time window
use_cases: Calculate C0 (active) residency percentage for specific CPU
Calculate C1/C2/C3 (sleep state) residency percentages
Analyze CPU power state distribution over time
Find how much time CPU spent in active vs sleep states
Debug CPU idle behavior and power management
Compare C-state residency across different CPUs
Analyze C-state behavior in specific time windows
etl_dataframes.notes:
LARGE dataset - always use time range filters to avoid memory issues
State values: 0=C0 (active), 1=C1 (light sleep), 2=C2 (deeper sleep), 3=C3 (deepest sleep)
Residency calculation requires summing durations and dividing by total time window
NOT a straightforward calculation - use the provided code logic

etl_dataframes.name: df_thread_interval
etl_dataframes.description: Thread execution intervals showing when and where each thread ran. Includes process, CPU, timing, priority, and QOS information. VERY LARGE dataset (1M+ rows)

columns.Start(s): Start time of thread execution in seconds
columns.End(s): End time of thread execution in seconds
columns.Switch-Out Reason: Why thread was switched out (WrQueue, Unknown, WrProcessInSwap, etc.)
columns.Switch-Out State: State when switched out (Waiting, Swapped, etc.)
columns.Ready(s): Time when thread became ready to run
columns.QOS: Quality of Service level (High, Medium, Low)
columns.Duration(s): Duration of thread execution in seconds

retrieval_code: # Process utilization: total CPU time for a process in time window
# Get QOS of a specific process
# Most common QOS on specific CPU
# When big cores (0-3) are used, what processes and QOS
# CPU usage per process (which CPUs did process run on)
# Top processes by CPU time in time window
use_cases: Calculate process CPU utilization in time window
Analyze which processes ran on which CPUs
Check QOS (Quality of Service) distribution for processes
Find most common QOS on specific CPUs
Analyze P-core vs E-core workload distribution
Get thread statistics and counts per process
Debug process scheduling and CPU affinity
Find top CPU-consuming processes
Analyze process priority and QOS patterns
Check which processes run on big cores (P-cores 0-3)
etl_dataframes.notes:
VERY LARGE dataset - ALWAYS use filters (process name, CPU, time range)
P-cores typically: CPU 0-3, E-cores: CPU 4-15 (varies by system)
Duration calculation: sum of all Duration(s) values for filtered data
Utilization % = (total_cpu_time / window_duration) * 100

etl_dataframes.name: df_processlifetime
etl_dataframes.description: Process lifecycle information - when processes started, ended, parent-child relationships, and command lines

columns.Command Line: Full command line used to start the process
columns.Parent Process: Name of parent process that spawned this process
columns.Hosted Service: Service name if process hosts a Windows service

retrieval_code: # Simple lookup of a process - get command line, duration, etc.
# Build process tree from origin to end
"""Build and display process tree showing parent-child relationships"""
if pd.notna(proc['Hosted Service']) and proc['Hosted Service']:
if pd.notna(proc['Parent PID']) and proc['Parent PID'] != 0:
# Usage: build tree for a specific process
# Find all child processes of a parent
if pd.notna(child['Hosted Service']) and child['Hosted Service']:
# Check if specific process ran during trace
print(f"{process_name} did NOT run during this trace")
# List processes still running at trace end (Status=259)
use_cases: Look up process command line and duration
Build process tree showing parent-child relationships
Trace process origin from child to parent to grandparent
Check if a specific process ran during the trace
Find all child processes of a parent process
Identify hosted Windows services
List processes still running at trace end
Find short-lived or long-lived processes
Debug process spawning and lifecycle issues
etl_dataframes.notes:
Status codes: 0=terminated normally, 259=still running at trace end, 1=terminated with error
Process tree building uses Parent Process and Parent PID for relationships
Command Line shows full execution path and arguments
Hosted Service column shows Windows service name if process hosts services

etl_dataframes.name: df_utilization_per_logical_summary
etl_dataframes.description: Utilization per logical core summary showing CPU time and utilization percentage for each CPU

columns.Duration(s): Total CPU active time in seconds

use_cases: Quick overview of CPU utilization across all cores
Identify heavily utilized or idle cores
Compare P-core vs E-core utilization
Check workload distribution across CPUs
Verify if workload is balanced or skewed to certain cores
etl_dataframes.notes:
Automatically retrieved with trace_summary statistics
One row per logical CPU/core in the system
Utilization(%) = (Duration(s) / trace_duration) * 100
Summary statistics generated from df_cpu_util dataframe
SUMMARY ONLY - For detailed time-window analysis, use df_cpu_util with time filtering

etl_dataframes.name: df_process_stats_summary
etl_dataframes.description: Process-level statistics summary showing runtime, utilization, switch-out count, concurrency, and hosted services for all processes

columns.Switch-Out Count: Number of times threads were switched out
columns.Hosted Service: Windows service name if process hosts a service

retrieval_code: # Automatically retrieve top 5 processes by utilization
use_cases: Quickly identify top CPU consuming processes
Get comprehensive statistics for specific process
Find multi-threaded processes (high concurrency)
Identify processes hosting Windows services
Check process runtime and context switch patterns
Compare process behavior across trace
etl_dataframes.notes:
Automatically retrieved - top 5 processes by utilization shown by default
One row per process (unique Process + PID combination)
Concurrency shows average concurrent thread count
Switch-Out Count indicates scheduling activity
Summary statistics generated from df_thread_interval dataframe
SUMMARY ONLY - For detailed time-window analysis, use df_thread_interval with time filtering

etl_dataframes.name: df_qos_per_process_summary
etl_dataframes.description: QOS (Quality of Service) distribution per process showing percentage of execution time at each QOS level (Eco, High, Low, Medium, Multimedia, Utility)

columns.Eco: Percentage of time at Eco QOS level
columns.High: Percentage of time at High QOS level
columns.Low: Percentage of time at Low QOS level
columns.Medium: Percentage of time at Medium QOS level
columns.Multimedia: Percentage of time at Multimedia QOS level
columns.Utility: Percentage of time at Utility QOS level

retrieval_code: # Automatically retrieve QOS distribution for top 5 processes
# Get QOS distribution for specific process
# Find processes running primarily at High QOS
# Find processes running primarily at Utility QOS (background)
use_cases: Check QOS distribution for top CPU consuming processes
Identify foreground vs background processes
Verify if high-priority processes run at High QOS
Find processes running at wrong QOS level
Debug QOS classification and scheduling behavior
Compare QOS patterns across different processes
etl_dataframes.notes:
Automatically retrieved - shows QOS distribution for top 5 processes by utilization
All QOS columns are percentages (0-100) and should sum to ~100% per process
High QOS = foreground/interactive, Utility QOS = background/low-priority
Medium QOS = user-interactive content, Multimedia QOS = media playback
Summary statistics generated from df_thread_interval dataframe
SUMMARY ONLY - For detailed time-window QOS analysis, use df_thread_interval with time filtering

etl_dataframes.name: df_qos_per_core_summary
etl_dataframes.description: QOS (Quality of Service) distribution per CPU/core showing percentage of execution time at each QOS level for each logical core

columns.Eco: Percentage of time at Eco QOS level
columns.High: Percentage of time at High QOS level
columns.Low: Percentage of time at Low QOS level
columns.Medium: Percentage of time at Medium QOS level
columns.Multimedia: Percentage of time at Multimedia QOS level
columns.Utility: Percentage of time at Utility QOS level

retrieval_code: # Compare P-cores vs E-cores QOS distribution
# Find cores running mostly High QOS (foreground work)
use_cases: Understand QOS distribution across all cores
Verify if High QOS work goes to P-cores vs E-cores
Check workload classification (foreground vs background) per core
Identify cores handling multimedia or interactive workloads
Debug hetero scheduling QOS-based core assignment
Compare QOS patterns between P-cores and E-cores
etl_dataframes.notes:
Automatically retrieved with trace_summary statistics
One row per logical CPU/core in the system
All QOS columns are percentages (0-100)
High QOS typically goes to P-cores, Utility QOS to E-cores in hetero systems
Summary statistics generated from df_thread_interval dataframe
SUMMARY ONLY - For detailed time-window QOS analysis per core, use df_thread_interval with time filtering

etl_dataframes.name: df_cpu_frequency_summary
etl_dataframes.description: Average CPU frequency per logical core showing the mean frequency in MHz for each CPU during the trace
use_cases: Quick overview of CPU frequency across all cores
Compare P-core vs E-core frequency behavior
Identify frequency throttling or thermal issues
Verify turbo boost activity on specific cores
Check if cores are running at expected frequencies
Debug performance issues related to CPU frequency
etl_dataframes.notes:
Automatically retrieved with trace_summary statistics
One row per logical CPU/core in the system
Frequency is averaged over the entire trace duration
P-cores typically run at higher frequencies than E-cores
Summary statistics generated from df_cpu_freq dataframe
SUMMARY ONLY - For detailed frequency analysis over time, use df_cpu_freq with time filtering

etl_dataframes.name: df_wlc
etl_dataframes.description: Workload Classification (WLC) events showing system workload type over time. Event-based data requiring resampling for time-based analysis.
retrieval_code: # Step 1: Resample to 1-second intervals with forward fill
trace_end = df_wlc['timestamp'].max()  # Or use max timestamp from trace
# Set timestamp as index and resample with forward fill
# Alternative: Using numpy histogram for time-weighted percentages
use_cases: Analyze workload classification distribution over trace
Check if system correctly identifies workload types
Debug workload classification transitions
Verify percentage of time in each workload class
Identify dominant workload patterns (bursty vs sustained)
Compare battery life vs performance workload modes
etl_dataframes.notes:
EVENT-BASED data - requires resampling to time-based for accurate statistics
WLC values: 0=Idle, 1=Battery Life, 2=Sustained, 3=Bursty, 4=VC (Video Conference)
Must use forward fill when resampling to 1-second intervals
Percentage calculation requires time-weighted analysis, not simple event counts
Typically 20-30 events per trace (sparse event-based changes)
Only populated when workload classification feature is active

etl_dataframes.name: df_containment_status
etl_dataframes.description: Containment enabled status extracted from HeteroParkingSelectionCount events

columns.ContainmentEnabled: Boolean/numeric flag indicating if containment is enabled

etl_dataframes.retrieval_code:
# Get containment status distribution
print(df_containment_status['ContainmentEnabled'].value_counts())

# Check if containment was enabled at any point
containment_active = df_containment_status['ContainmentEnabled'].any()
print(f"Containment active: {containment_active}")

# Get percentage of time with containment enabled (if populated with time data)
if len(df_containment_status) > 0:
    enabled_pct = (df_containment_status['ContainmentEnabled'].sum() / len(df_containment_status)) * 100
    print(f"Containment enabled: {enabled_pct:.1f}% of events")
use_cases: Check if CPU containment feature is enabled in the trace
Verify containment status during hetero parking selection
Debug heterogeneous CPU containment behavior
Track containment policy changes over time
Correlate containment status with performance or power metrics
etl_dataframes.notes:
Extracted from Microsoft-Windows-Kernel-Processor-Power/HeteroParkingSelectionCount/win:Info events
ContainmentEnabled indicates whether CPU containment is active
Number of rows depends on frequency of HeteroParkingSelectionCount events
Only populated when hetero parking selection events are present in trace
Related to heterogeneous CPU scheduling and core parking containment
